title: Dynamical cavity method for continuous-time complex systems on sparse random graphs
authors: Fernando L. Metz, Isaac Pérez Castillo
abstract: Dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) reduces dense high-dimensional disordered dynamics to a self-consistent effective stochastic process. For sparse and heterogeneous networks, however, local fields contain finitely many strong inputs, so the Gaussian closure mechanisms of dense DMFT need not apply. We develop a continuous-time cavity derivation of sparse-network DMFT at the level of path measures for stochastic dynamics with general pairwise interactions on sparse random graphs. The cavity equations are exact on trees and yield the finite-time thermodynamic description on locally tree-like graphs. They show explicitly how reciprocity changes dynamical closure: fully directed graphs recover the sparse directed path-probability equation, whereas reciprocal or bidirected edges require conditional path kernels driven by the imposed history of the receiving node. Ensemble averaging gives laws over path-probability messages, with barycenters and higher-message moments closing by multilinearity and independence of incoming branches. A causal discrete-time derivation yields the corresponding population-dynamics representation, distinguishing trajectory populations for directed graphs from conditional branch-law or finite-depth tree populations for reciprocal graphs. We also formulate finite-memory numerical closures and test them in an additive-input recurrent neural network specialization. Finally, high-connectivity limits are obtained as projections of the sparse path-measure theory, clarifying when dense drift, noise, and response channels reduce to standard low-dimensional DMFT and when path-level descriptions remain essential.
summary: This work extends Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) to stochastic processes on sparse random graphs by developing a path-measure based cavity method. It rigorously distinguishes the mathematical requirements for closure based on graph reciprocity, showing that bidirected edges necessitate tracking complex conditional history kernels. The theory provides tools to analyze both finite-time and high-connectivity limits of such complex systems.
main_problem: The paper develops a continuous-time cavity derivation for Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) applied to stochastic dynamics on sparse, heterogeneous random graphs.
main_result: It establishes that graph reciprocity dictates the closure structure of the path probability equations, requiring conditional kernels for bidirected edges, while providing systematic methods for high-connectivity limits.
method: The core method is developing cavity equations at the level of path measures, which are then translated into population dynamics representations for finite-time analysis.
model_or_system: The system models continuous degrees of freedom evolving via coupled stochastic differential equations (SDEs) on sparse random graphs. This framework generalizes standard DMFT to handle local field structures arising from sparsity and general pairwise interactions.
key_observables: Path measures, path probabilities, message laws, barycenters, and higher-message moments are key observables used to characterize the system's dynamics.
important_parameters: Graph structure (directed vs. reciprocal), connectivity regime (sparse vs. high-connectivity limit), and time scale ($t$ or finite depth $M$).
main_assumptions: The central approximation relies on factorizing the joint cavity marginal, which is exact only on trees but forms the basis for the thermodynamic limit on locally tree-like graphs.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes; however, the text refers to figures illustrating mean and variance tracking in simulations (e.g., Eq. 186).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the general path measure formulation on fixed graphs, deriving cavity equations via factorization approximations, analyzing the implications of graph reciprocity for closure structure, and finally projecting these results onto high-connectivity limits.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous extension of DMFT to non-Gaussian, sparse network dynamics, which is crucial for modeling complex systems like recurrent neural networks or disordered physical materials where local interactions dominate.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Finite-depth causal tree for the bidirected population-dynamics construc- tion. The root history is generated from neighbour histories one time layer shorter; each neighbour history is generated from its own descendants with one further time layer removed. The recursion terminates at leaves drawn from the initial distribu- tion. The schematic is drawn for the zero-noise, constant-coupling additive-input example used to make the causal structure explicit. In the general pairwise formu- lation, ordinary source histories are replaced by the corresponding imposed-history branch path laws.

Figure 2: Comparison between direct finite-graph simulations and finite-depth pop- ulation dynamics for undirected random graphs with Poisson degree distribution of mean c. Coupling strengths are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with unit mean and unit variance. The time-discretization parameter is ∆= 0.1. Crosses denote direct finite-graph simulations of the discretized microscopic dynamics with N = 15000, while the remaining symbols denote the finite-depth population- dynamics construction with population size Npop = 105. The left panel shows the recurrent neural-network model and the right panel shows the Lotka–Volterra model with immigration rate λ = 10−2. Error bars are not shown.

Figure 3: Comparison between direct finite-graph simulations and finite-depth popu- lation dynamics for undirected regular random graphs with fixed degree c. Coupling strengths are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with mean 〈J〉= −1 and unit variance. The time-discretization parameter is ∆= 0.1. Crosses denote direct finite- graph simulations of the discretized microscopic dynamics with N = 15000, while the remaining symbols denote the finite-depth population-dynamics construction with population size Npop = 105. The left panel shows the recurrent neural-network model and the right panel shows the Lotka–Volterra model with immigration rate λ = 10−2. Error bars are not shown.

Figure 4: Graph-level view of the moving-window approximation. In the exact finite graph, the history window of a root i advances while the same rooted marked neigh- bourhood Ei is retained. In the moving-window population closure, the particle label a carries the shifted window, but the local marked environment used to generate the next endpoint is sampled anew. Thus the approximation is not in the one-step up- date, but in replacing the joint law of a window and its persistent rooted environment by a marginal window law together with an annealed environment draw.

Figure 5: Comparison between direct finite-graph simulations and three finite- memory closures for the additive-input RNN on undirected Poisson graphs. The memory depth is fixed to L = 3. Columns show, from left to right, the rolling cavity, the root-quenched rolling cavity, and the endpoint mean-corrected rolling cavity. The top row shows the mean m(t), and the bottom row shows the second moment q(t). Solid curves are direct finite-graph simulations of Eq. (138); dashed curves are the corresponding finite-memory closure. Colours denote c = 2,4,8. The simulations use ∆= 10−2, M = 300, N = 15000, population size Npop = 105, initial condition x0 i = 0.5, symmetric reciprocal couplings Ji j =...
